In FIFO replacement algorithm, more pages are given, more page fault happen. This wired thing call Belady's Anomaly.

Belady's Anomaly is a counter-intuitive phenomenon where having more available slots to load pages in a page replacement algorithm actually leads to more page faults. Per example, the same sequence of page requests will lead to more page faults if you have 4 slots to load them than if you have 3. It happens in the FIFO algorithm.

Belady's anomaly is the curious behavior when allocating more frames to a process induces a increase a page fault.

question_context.prompt: Explain Belady’s Anomaly in the context of page replacement algorithms. Which replacement algorithm has this anomaly?
question_context.reference_answer:
a. Belady’s Anomaly occurs when increasing the number of page frames leads to more page faults.
b. The FIFO replacement has Belady’s Anomaly. It occurs because FIFO doesn't consider access patterns; older pages might be replaced despite future use.
